briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for June 12, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Global Climate Crisis and Urgent Call for Emergency Response
sections.content: According to news released today, the El Niño phenomenon officially began on June 12, 2026, and the global climate crisis is intensifying, with sea ice the size of France lost off the west coast of Antarctica and temperatures recorded 20°C higher than average [49, 50]. These climate changes are having widespread impacts, affecting World Cup matches [24, 29], Swiss Alpine huts [40], and even fish migration routes [30], and the economic burden is increasing to the extent that many Americans cite climate change as the cause of their rising expenses [26]. Vermont is preparing for summer in anticipation of intensified extreme weather events [28], and domestically, large-scale disasters are becoming more frequent amidst the climate crisis, making emergency responses urgent, such as the ongoing drills for complex summer disasters [2]. Furthermore, there was a warning that without climate change response, the risk of wildfires in the Gyeongbuk region would increase [3].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Cooperation and Policy Trends
sections.content: The international community continues its cooperation and discussions to address climate change. The UN General Assembly adopted a resolution welcoming the International Court of Justice's (ICJ) advisory opinion on climate change, thereby strengthening international legal efforts [31]. South Korea and Peru held a joint committee on climate change cooperation to explore greenhouse gas reduction collaboration [4], and the Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA) signed a sub-loan agreement for climate change adaptation infrastructure development, supporting adaptation efforts in developing countries [6]. Meanwhile, London Climate Action Week was held to discuss climate action for future generations [36], and with the advent of El Niño, Malaysia is exploring innovative solutions such as cultivated meat to address food insecurity [38]. Scientific analyses were also presented, indicating that climate change redistributes sunlight across the globe and that the speed of topographic water flow in Europe is reshaping nitrogen pollution risks [33, 35]. However, criticisms were raised that climate change response efforts often exclude indigenous peoples [25], and that major culprits of climate change not addressed in international policies exist [27]. Furthermore, policy debates continue, with a bill limiting climate change-related lawsuits being signed in Louisiana [32].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Boosting Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: Domestically, efforts to achieve carbon neutrality and transition to renewable energy are accelerating in various sectors. Cheongju City provided incentives totaling approximately 120 million KRW to 8,000 households participating in carbon neutrality practices [14], and Gumi City spread the culture of carbon neutrality practices through its Environment Day commemorative event [13]. The Soha 1-dong Residents' Autonomy Committee in Gwangmyeong City held a 'Jupking' (picking up trash while jogging) event [15], and citizens are practicing carbon neutrality in their daily lives through bicycle commuting [7] and gardening [10, 11]. In the industrial sector, the impending enforcement of the K-Steel Act has initiated movements towards carbon neutrality [8], and KEPCO Research Institute is fulfilling its role as a first mover in carbon neutrality [9]. In the renewable energy sector, heat pumps are being recognized as renewable energy, leading major corporations like Samsung and LG to enter the market [16, 17], and grid-forming technology, which overcomes the intermittency of renewable energy, is emerging as key to off-grid green hydrogen production [19]. The Korea Energy Agency is also strengthening international cooperation by launching agri-photovoltaic and greenhouse gas reduction ODA projects with Indonesia [21]. Despite these efforts, it was also pointed out that expanding the power grid is crucial for achieving the 100GW renewable energy target [20].

sections.title: 🌱 Asia's Renewable Energy Milestones and Future Scientific Questions
sections.content: Significant milestones have been achieved in the renewable energy transition across Asia. According to analysis, solar power generation in Asia has surpassed gas power for the first time, becoming the continent's third-largest power source [51]. This can be interpreted as a positive signal for Asia's changing energy mix and carbon reduction efforts. Furthermore, EDF has signed an official partnership for the renewable energy operation of Glasgow stadiums in 2026, expanding the use of renewable energy in the sports sector [18]. Meanwhile, scientific questions have also been raised regarding the impact of climate change on increased plant photosynthesis and the potential changes in carbon storage resulting from it, emphasizing the need for continuous research into the complex interactions of the climate system [1, 5].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has once again highlighted the severity of climate threats, while also delivering hopeful news regarding various carbon neutrality efforts and renewable energy transitions underway both domestically and internationally. Only sustained interest and participation can safeguard our collective future. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Antarctica’s west coast missing an area of sea ice the size of France as temperatures peak 20C above average
articles.description: Exclusive A vast area of the Bellingshausen Sea should be covered by sea ice by now, with one expert calling the loss of ice ‘depressing’Antarctica’s west coast is missing an area of winter sea ice the size of France, sparking concerns for threatened penguins other marine life and global sea levels.One expert said the loss of ice in the Bellingshausen Sea was “depressing” and the failure of ice to form could have intensified a heatwave over the continent’s peninsular last week that saw daytime t

summary.phenomenon: An area of sea ice the size of France failed to form in the Bellingshausen Sea off the west coast of Antarctica. Last week, a heatwave occurred on the Antarctic Peninsula, with daytime temperatures reaching a record high of 20°C above average.
summary.cause: While the direct scientific cause of the sea ice loss is not explicitly mentioned in the article, it is noted that the failure of sea ice to form may have intensified the heatwave on the Antarctic Peninsula.
summary.outlook: The loss of sea ice raises concerns for marine life, including penguins, and could contribute to global sea level rise.
